treatments.name: Induction Cisplatin/Etoposide followed by Definitive Chemoradiation (Response-Adapted)

evidence_level.rationale: Large retrospective series (Amit et al., 95 patients at MD Anderson) with consistent results across multiple institutions. Not Phase 3 RCT but the largest SNUC-specific evidence base available for this ultra-rare tumor

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 81% for IC responders receiving CRT (Amit 2019). 5-year DSS 81% in responders. Overall cohort 5-year OS 56%. IPD meta-analysis shows 72.6% 5-year OS with IC vs 44.5% without (Burggraf 2024)

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin and etoposide universally available at all cancer centers. IMRT widely available. No special infrastructure required beyond standard oncology

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3/4 hematologic toxicity 34% (neutropenia 20%, thrombocytopenia 11%). Grade 3/4 non-hematologic 26%. Hearing impairment 25%. Significant mucositis with concurrent CRT. Manageable with supportive care

biomarker_match.rationale: Applies to all SNUC regardless of IDH2 status. IC response-adapted approach particularly valuable as Ki-67 80% suggests chemosensitivity to platinum/etoposide given high proliferative index

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin forms DNA crosslinks causing replication arrest and apoptosis. Etoposide inhibits topoisomerase II, blocking DNA unwinding. Sequential approach uses IC response to guide definitive therapy: responders receive CRT (cisplatin + 70 Gy IMRT), non-responders undergo salvage surgery + adjuvant RT.

key_evidence.study_name: Amit et al. Induction Chemotherapy Response as a Guide for Treatment Optimization in SNUC

treatments.availability: Universally available — standard oncology drugs and IMRT

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab + Cisplatin + 5-FU (First-Line Immunotherapy-Chemotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-048 Phase 3 RCT (882 patients, 5-year follow-up) established pembrolizumab+chemo as first-line for R/M HNSCC. Not SNUC-specific but biologically rational extrapolation. Case reports of checkpoint inhibitor responses in SNUC

survival_benefit.rationale: KEYNOTE-048: 5-year OS 16% vs 5.2% (HR 0.72 total population). In CPS>=20: HR 0.62. For SNUC specifically, case reports show durable responses to immunotherapy. IDH2 inhibition may activate cGAS-STING pathway, potentially synergizing with ICI

accessibility.rationale: Pembrolizumab FDA approved for first-line R/M HNSCC per KEYNOTE-048. Off-label use for SNUC requires oncologist rationale but pembrolizumab widely available. CPS testing standard

safety_profile.rationale: Immune-related AEs: pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies. Chemotherapy toxicity additive. Lower overall toxicity than EXTREME regimen. Grade 3+ AEs in ~20-25% with single-agent pembrolizumab

biomarker_match.rationale: Benefit correlates with PD-L1 CPS score (unknown for this patient — should be tested). IDH2-mutant tumors may have altered immune microenvironment. Ki-67 80% suggests high neoantigen load which correlates with ICI response

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab blocks PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, restoring T cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity. Combined with cisplatin (DNA crosslinks) and 5-FU (thymidylate synthase inhibition) for synergistic cytotoxic and immunogenic cell death. Chemotherapy-induced immunogenic cell death may enhance pembrolizumab efficacy.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for R/M HNSCC first-line (off-label for SNUC)

treatments.name: Trimodality Therapy: Induction Chemotherapy + Surgery + Adjuvant Chemoradiation

evidence_level.rationale: International multicenter retrospective study (485 patients, Cancers 2026) and multiple institutional series support trimodality. No RCT exists but consistent outcomes across 15 centers

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 47.2% overall (multicenter cohort). For T4a patients with surgery, R0 resection yields best outcomes. 2/3/4-year OS 75%/50%/48% in trimodality series. IC non-responders benefit most from surgery (5-yr DSS 39% vs 0% with CRT alone)

accessibility.rationale: Requires experienced skull base surgical team (open craniofacial resection or endoscopic approach). Available at major academic centers. T4a with N1 is typically resectable at specialized centers

safety_profile.rationale: Craniofacial resection carries significant morbidity: CSF leak, wound complications, orbital complications. Potential orbital exenteration for T4a. Combined toxicity from all three modalities is substantial

biomarker_match.rationale: Universally applicable to SNUC. Particularly valuable for IC non-responders where surgery is the only salvage option. T4aN1 stage makes surgical approach reasonable if IC response is suboptimal

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Multimodal approach: induction chemotherapy debulks tumor and tests chemosensitivity; surgical resection (endoscopic or open craniofacial) removes gross disease with negative margins; adjuvant chemoradiation (cisplatin + 60-66 Gy) eradicates microscopic residual disease and treats the N1 neck.

key_evidence.study_name: International Multi-Center Retrospective Cohort Study of SNUC

treatments.availability: Available at major skull base surgery centers

treatments.name: TPF Induction (Docetaxel/Cisplatin/5-FU) followed by CRT

evidence_level.rationale: Case series and comprehensive review for SNUC (MDPI Cancers 2023). TPF is Phase 3-validated for HNSCC induction (TAX 323/324) but only retrospective series for SNUC. 21/95 patients received docetaxel-based IC in Amit et al.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 66% for IC followed by CRT (meta-analysis). Case reports of complete pathological response. Higher response rate may be achievable with triplet vs doublet for highly proliferative Ki-67 80% tumors

accessibility.rationale: Docetaxel, cisplatin, and 5-FU all universally available. Standard oncology regimen. No special infrastructure required

safety_profile.rationale: TPF is more toxic than cisplatin/etoposide doublet. 6% toxic death rate reported in 113-patient TPF series. Higher febrile neutropenia risk. G-CSF support mandatory. Increased liver toxicity risk

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability. Docetaxel adds mitotic inhibition to platinum synergy. Ki-67 80% suggests high proliferative fraction that should be particularly susceptible to anti-mitotic agents

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Triplet combination: docetaxel stabilizes microtubules (mitotic arrest), cisplatin forms DNA crosslinks (replication block), 5-FU inhibits thymidylate synthase (DNA synthesis inhibition). Three complementary mechanisms of cytotoxicity maximize tumor cell kill before definitive local therapy.

key_evidence.study_name: Induction Chemotherapy for Locoregionally Advanced Sinonasal SCC and SNUC: Comprehensive Review

treatments.name: Proton Beam Therapy (Post-Surgical or Definitive)

evidence_level.rationale: Multi-institutional retrospective series and 2025 systematic review/meta-analysis. 36/210 patients in international SNUC cohort received proton therapy. No RCT vs IMRT but consistent favorable outcomes

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year LC 80%, 5-year OS 59% in sinonasal cancer proton series. Proton vs 3D-CRT: HR 0.22 (p=0.003) for OS in univariable analysis. Superior to 3D-CRT and comparable/superior to IMRT for sinonasal tumors

accessibility.rationale: Proton therapy centers limited worldwide (approximately 100 centers globally). Not available in most community settings. Insurance coverage may be challenging. Used in 17.1% of international SNUC cohort

safety_profile.rationale: Reduced dose to adjacent normal structures (brain, optic apparatus, contralateral eye). Late grade 3+ toxicity 22%. CNS necrosis 6%, vision loss 3.5%. Less late toxicity vs photon RT for skull base tumors

biomarker_match.rationale: Applicable to all SNUC regardless of molecular subtype. Particularly beneficial for T4a tumors adjacent to critical structures (orbit, brain, optic nerves). Dosimetric advantage for skull base proximity

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beams deposit energy in a Bragg peak, allowing precise dose concentration at tumor depth with rapid dose fall-off beyond the target. Minimizes radiation to adjacent critical structures (optic nerves, chiasm, brain parenchyma, contralateral orbit) compared to photon-based IMRT, reducing late toxicity in the sinonasal region.

key_evidence.study_name: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Proton Therapy in Sinonasal Cancers

treatments.availability: Available at specialized proton therapy centers

treatments.name: Endoscopic Endonasal Surgery (Minimally Invasive Approach)

evidence_level.rationale: Multiple institutional series comparing endoscopic vs open craniofacial approaches for sinonasal tumors. International SNUC cohort showed comparable outcomes. 2024 meta-analysis included endoscopic approaches

survival_benefit.rationale: R0 resection rates comparable to open approach in appropriately selected cases. 5-year OS with surgery 47.2% in international cohort. Key is achieving negative margins — approach is secondary to resection quality

accessibility.rationale: Requires experienced endoscopic skull base surgeon. Available at major academic centers. Less resource-intensive than open craniofacial resection. Growing availability as technique matures

safety_profile.rationale: Reduced morbidity vs open craniofacial resection: no facial incisions, shorter recovery, lower CSF leak rate with vascularized flap reconstruction. Lower blood loss. Faster return to adjuvant therapy

biomarker_match.rationale: Applicable to all SNUC. T4a stage may be endoscopically resectable depending on specific extension pattern. Must assess orbital/dural invasion extent for approach selection

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Endoscopic endonasal approach uses nasal corridors and endoscopic visualization to resect sinonasal tumors with minimal external incisions. Permits detailed visualization of tumor margins under endoscopic magnification. Vascularized nasoseptal flap reconstruction reduces CSF leak risk. May be combined with craniotomy for selected T4a cases.

key_evidence.study_name: International Multi-Center Retrospective Cohort Study (surgical subgroup)

treatments.availability: Available at academic skull base surgery centers

treatments.name: Neoadjuvant Pembrolizumab + Chemotherapy (Curative Intent)

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-689 Phase 3 (neoadjuvant/adjuvant pembrolizumab in LA HNSCC, NEJM 2025) showed EFS benefit. Small series in sinonasal SCC. A clinical trial is testing pembrolizumab+chemo as neoadjuvant in sinonasal carcinoma

survival_benefit.rationale: KEYNOTE-689: neoadjuvant pembro improved EFS in LA HNSCC. In R/M sinonasal SCC Phase II: ORR 60%, DCR 100% with pembrolizumab+nab-paclitaxel+cisplatin. Neoadjuvant approach may downstage T4a tumors

accessibility.rationale: Pembrolizumab available but neoadjuvant use in sinonasal tumors is investigational. Clinical trial enrollment required for protocol-directed approach. Emerging data may expand indications

safety_profile.rationale: Combined immune and chemotherapy toxicity. Immune-related AEs may complicate subsequent surgery. Risk of surgical delay if immune toxicity occurs. Manageable with experienced multidisciplinary teams

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 CPS score should guide patient selection. Ki-67 80% suggests high proliferative tumor that may generate more neoantigens, potentially enhancing immunotherapy response

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neoadjuvant combination of PD-1 checkpoint blockade with platinum-based chemotherapy before definitive surgery. Chemotherapy induces immunogenic cell death, releasing tumor antigens that prime anti-tumor T cells, which are then unleashed by concurrent PD-1 blockade. Aims to achieve pathological downstaging before resection.
biomarker_requirements: MSI-H/dMMR status may predict exceptional response
notable_side_effects: Potential surgical delay due to irAEs
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial / Investigational for sinonasal carcinoma

treatments.name: Enasidenib (IDH2 Inhibitor, 100 mg Daily) — Targeted Monotherapy

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial (NCT06176989) currently enrolling 25 patients for IDH2-mutated sinonasal tumors. No results yet from sinonasal indication. 40% ORR in IDH2-mutant AML (AG221-C-001 trial) establishes proof of concept

survival_benefit.rationale: In AML: median OS 19.7 months with enasidenib, CR rate 19.3%. Sinonasal tumor outcomes unknown but IDH2-mutant SNUC has better prognosis than wild-type (5-year OS 43% vs lower). Targeted approach may improve on historical median OS ~20 months

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved only for relapsed/refractory IDH2-mutant AML. For sinonasal tumors, available only through NCT06176989 clinical trial (NCI-sponsored) or compassionate use. Off-label use possible but insurance coverage unlikely

safety_profile.rationale: Oral agent with manageable toxicity profile. Main AEs: hyperbilirubinemia (38%), nausea (23%), decreased appetite (19%). IDH differentiation syndrome reported in AML but less relevant in solid tumors. Generally well-tolerated

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets IDH2 mutation — the defining molecular alteration in this patient's tumor. IDH2 R172 mutations found in 20-88% of SNUC. This patient's IDH2-mutant status makes them an ideal candidate. Reduces oncometabolite 2-HG by up to 98%

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Enasidenib selectively inhibits mutant IDH2 enzyme, blocking conversion of alpha-ketoglutarate to the oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG). Reduces 2-HG levels by up to 98%, reversing aberrant DNA/histone methylation and restoring cellular differentiation. Oral, continuous dosing at 100 mg daily in 28-day cycles.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Enasidenib in IDH2-Mutated Malignant Sinonasal and Skull Base Tumors
key_evidence.journal: Journal of Clinical Oncology (ASCO 2024 Abstract TPS3183)

biomarker_requirements: IDH2 mutation (R172S, R172T, R172G, or R172M)
notable_side_effects: IDH differentiation syndrome (rare in solid tumors)
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial — NCT06176989 (NCI Phase II, recruiting)

treatments.name: Dual Checkpoint Inhibition: Nivolumab + Ipilimumab (Anti-PD-1/Anti-CTLA-4)

evidence_level.rationale: Case report evidence only for SNUC (Trinh 2024). Durable response documented in metastatic SNUC after single-agent pembrolizumab failure. Nivolumab+ipilimumab approved for other solid tumors (melanoma, RCC, NSCLC)

survival_benefit.rationale: Case report showed durable and dramatic response in metastatic SNUC with addition of ipilimumab + celecoxib after pembrolizumab resistance. No survival statistics available for SNUC population. In melanoma, nivo+ipi achieves 5-year OS 52%

accessibility.rationale: Both nivolumab and ipilimumab FDA approved for multiple tumor types. Off-label use for SNUC feasible with oncologist justification. Widely available at cancer centers

safety_profile.rationale: Dual checkpoint inhibition carries significant immune toxicity: grade 3/4 AEs in 55-59% (CheckMate-067). Colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies more frequent than monotherapy. Requires close monitoring

biomarker_match.rationale: Applicable regardless of IDH2 status. May synergize with IDH2 inhibition via cGAS-STING pathway activation and T cell reinvigoration. Ki-67 80% suggests high tumor mutational burden favoring ICI response

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Dual checkpoint blockade: nivolumab blocks PD-1 on T cells (preventing exhaustion), while ipilimumab blocks CTLA-4 (enhancing T cell priming and activation). Combined blockade provides complementary immune activation at different stages of the anti-tumor immune response.

key_evidence.study_name: Case report: Durable response to checkpoint inhibition + COX-2 inhibitor in metastatic SNUC

biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 testing recommended but not required
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for multiple tumor types (off-label for SNUC)

treatments.name: Concurrent Intra-Arterial Cisplatin + Radiation (RADPLAT)

evidence_level.rationale: Case reports and small series in unresectable SNUC. RADPLAT technique described with concurrent IA cisplatin and 67 Gy RT. Promising outcomes in select patients but limited evidence base

survival_benefit.rationale: Case report of unresectable T4N0 SNUC treated with 4 cycles weekly IA cisplatin + 67 Gy achieved durable local control. 2-year OS 64% and 2-year PFS 43% in a small series. May be option for unresectable T4a disease

accessibility.rationale: Intra-arterial chemotherapy delivery requires interventional radiology expertise and specialized catheterization. Limited to academic centers with this capability. Not widely practiced

safety_profile.rationale: Intra-arterial delivery concentrates cisplatin locally, potentially reducing systemic toxicity but with catheter-related complications. Sodium thiosulfate used for systemic cisplatin neutralization. Stroke risk from arterial catheterization

biomarker_match.rationale: Applicable to all SNUC regardless of molecular profile. Particularly relevant for unresectable T4a disease where maximizing local drug delivery is critical

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin delivered directly into the tumor-feeding arteries achieves much higher local drug concentrations than IV administration. Concurrent sodium thiosulfate IV neutralizes systemic cisplatin, reducing nephrotoxicity. Combined with external beam radiation for synergistic DNA damage.

key_evidence.study_name: Survival in Unresectable SNUC Treated with Concurrent Intra-Arterial Cisplatin and Radiation

treatments.availability: Available at select academic centers with interventional radiology

treatments.name: Vorasidenib (Dual IDH1/IDH2 Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 INDIGO trial validated vorasidenib in IDH-mutant low-grade glioma (NEJM 2023). No sinonasal-specific data. Brain-penetrant design may be advantageous for skull base tumors with intracranial extension

survival_benefit.rationale: INDIGO trial: median PFS 27.7 months vs 11.1 months (HR 0.39, p<0.001) in grade 2 glioma. PFS improvement dramatic but in a very different tumor biology. Sinonasal tumor response unknown. Dual IDH1/2 inhibition may be more potent than IDH2-only inhibition

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for IDH-mutant grade 2 glioma only (August 2024). No approval or active trial for sinonasal tumors. Would require compassionate use or off-label access. Insurance coverage very unlikely

safety_profile.rationale: Well-tolerated oral agent. INDIGO: grade 3+ AEs only 22.8%. Main AEs: elevated ALT/AST (9.6% grade 3), fatigue, headache. No differentiation syndrome in solid tumor setting

biomarker_match.rationale: Inhibits both mutant IDH1 and IDH2 — directly relevant to this patient's IDH2 mutation. Broader target coverage than enasidenib alone. Brain-penetrant formulation valuable for T4a tumors near skull base

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Vorasidenib is an oral, brain-penetrant dual inhibitor of mutant IDH1 and IDH2 enzymes. Blocks production of 2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG) oncometabolite, reversing epigenetic dysregulation (DNA/histone hypermethylation) and restoring normal cellular differentiation. Brain penetrance makes it uniquely suited for skull base tumors.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for IDH-mutant grade 2 glioma only — off-label/compassionate use for sinonasal

treatments.name: IDH2 Inhibitor + Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Combination

evidence_level.rationale: Preclinical rationale only. 2025 study showed IDH2 inhibition activates cGAS-STING pathway, promoting CD8+ T cell infiltration. No clinical trial combining IDH2 inhibitor + ICI in sinonasal tumors exists. Theoretical synergy well-supported

survival_benefit.rationale: Preclinical: IDH2 inhibition increases STING expression, CXCL10/CCL5 production, and CD8+ T cell recruitment. Mouse models show significant tumor growth inhibition with combined approach. Translational potential high for IDH2-mutant tumors

accessibility.rationale: No clinical trial exists. Would require compassionate use of enasidenib/vorasidenib combined with off-label pembrolizumab/nivolumab. Innovative oncologist at academic center needed to design protocol

safety_profile.rationale: Both drug classes individually well-tolerated. Additive toxicity expected to be manageable. IDH2 inhibitor (oral, mild AEs) plus ICI (immune-related AEs). No overlapping toxicity profiles suggest feasibility

biomarker_match.rationale: Scientifically rationalized specifically for IDH2-mutant tumors. This patient's IDH2 mutation is the direct mechanistic basis for the combination. STING pathway activation by IDH2 inhibition creates hot tumor microenvironment for ICI

treatments.mechanism_of_action: IDH2 inhibition (enasidenib) reduces 2-HG accumulation, demethylating the STING promoter and restoring cGAS-STING innate immune signaling. This triggers type I interferon production, CXCL10/CCL5 chemokine secretion, and CD8+ T cell recruitment — converting immunologically cold tumors to hot. Concurrent anti-PD-1 therapy (pembrolizumab) then unleashes the recruited T cells against tumor.

key_evidence.study_name: Targeting IDH2 Promotes Antitumor Immunity through Epigenetic Activation of cGAS-STING Pathway

notable_side_effects: Combined immune-related and IDH2 inhibitor AEs
Hepatotoxicity monitoring required for both agents
treatments.availability: Research Only — no active clinical trial

treatments.name: PARP Inhibitors (Olaparib) for IDH2-Mutant SNUC

evidence_level.rationale: Preclinical rationale only. IDH2 mutation-derived 2-HG inhibits homologous recombination repair enzymes, creating a BRCAness phenotype. PARP inhibitors exploit this synthetic lethality. Review in PMC9918684 discusses this approach

survival_benefit.rationale: No clinical data in sinonasal tumors. PARP inhibitors effective in BRCA-mutant cancers (breast, ovarian). Theoretical benefit in IDH2-mutant tumors via 2-HG-induced HR deficiency. Early preclinical data supports concept

accessibility.rationale: Olaparib FDA approved for BRCA-mutant breast, ovarian, pancreatic, prostate cancers. Off-label use requires IDH2-mutation rationale. Available at most oncology centers

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized safety profile from extensive use in BRCA-mutant cancers. Main AEs: anemia, fatigue, nausea. Risk of MDS/AML with long-term use (~1%). Oral dosing convenient

biomarker_match.rationale: IDH2 mutation creates 2-HG accumulation which inhibits HR repair — establishing the BRCAness phenotype that PARP inhibitors exploit. Direct mechanistic rationale for this patient's IDH2-mutant tumor

treatments.mechanism_of_action: PARP inhibitors block poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase, preventing single-strand DNA break repair. In IDH2-mutant tumors, 2-HG accumulation impairs homologous recombination repair by inhibiting KDM4A/KDM4B histone demethylases, creating a BRCAness phenotype. This synthetic lethality traps PARP on DNA, converting single-strand breaks into lethal double-strand breaks in HR-deficient tumor cells.

key_evidence.study_name: IDH2-Mutated Sinonasal Tumors: A Review (discussion of PARP inhibitor rationale)

biomarker_requirements: HR deficiency testing may support usage
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for BRCA-mutant cancers — off-label for sinonasal

treatments.name: Hypomethylating Agents (Decitabine/Azacitidine) for IDH2-Mutant SNUC

evidence_level.rationale: Mechanistic rationale from IDH2-mutant tumor biology (2-HG causes DNA hypermethylation). Decitabine approved for MDS/AML. Enasidenib + azacitidine combination tested in AML (Nature 2021). No sinonasal-specific data

survival_benefit.rationale: In AML: enasidenib + azacitidine achieved ORR 74% in IDH2-mutant newly diagnosed AML (Dinardo 2021). Translational relevance to sinonasal tumors uncertain. May restore differentiation in undifferentiated carcinoma

accessibility.rationale: Decitabine and azacitidine FDA approved for MDS/AML. Off-label use for solid tumors feasible. Available at most oncology centers. Cost manageable

safety_profile.rationale: Manageable hematologic toxicity (cytopenias). Nausea, fatigue common. Better tolerated than intensive chemotherapy. Long-term safety well-characterized from MDS/AML experience

biomarker_match.rationale: IDH2 mutation drives DNA hypermethylation — the direct target of hypomethylating agents. Logical approach to reverse the epigenetic driver of tumorigenesis. May restore normal differentiation in undifferentiated carcinoma

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Hypomethylating agents (decitabine, azacitidine) incorporate into DNA and inhibit DNA methyltransferases, reducing aberrant CpG island hypermethylation. In IDH2-mutant tumors, 2-HG accumulation causes global DNA hypermethylation — HMAs directly counteract this epigenetic dysregulation, potentially restoring tumor suppressor gene expression and normal differentiation.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for MDS/AML — off-label for sinonasal tumors

treatments.name: Anti-PD-L1 + IL-15 Superagonist (N-803) + NK Cell Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: Preclinical study only (2024). SNUC cell line experiments showed enhanced NK-cell killing with anti-PD-L1 + IL-15 superagonist combination. No clinical trial for this specific combination in SNUC

survival_benefit.rationale: Preclinical data showed enhanced NK-cell cytotoxicity against SNUC cells with the combination. IL-15 superagonist (N-803) has shown activity in bladder cancer (FDA approved 2024). Translational gap significant

accessibility.rationale: N-803 (nogapendekin alfa) FDA approved only for BCG-unresponsive bladder cancer. NK cell therapy requires specialized manufacturing. No trial for sinonasal tumors. Research-only availability

safety_profile.rationale: IL-15 superagonist can cause cytokine release syndrome. NK cell therapy carries infusion-related risks. Anti-PD-L1 immune AEs additive. Complex three-agent regimen with limited safety data in this combination

biomarker_match.rationale: SNUC-specific preclinical data provides direct relevance. NK cell activity is MHC-unrestricted, potentially effective even in tumors with altered MHC expression from IDH2-driven epigenetic changes

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Triple combination: anti-PD-L1 antibody blocks immune checkpoint on tumor cells, IL-15 superagonist (N-803) activates and expands NK cells and CD8+ T cells in vivo, and exogenous NK cell therapy provides additional direct cytotoxicity against SNUC cells. Preclinical data shows synergistic enhancement of NK-cell killing of SNUC cells with this combination.

key_evidence.study_name: Targeting Sinonasal Undifferentiated Carcinoma with a Combinatory Immunotherapy Approach

biomarker_requirements: NK cell receptor ligand expression assessment
treatments.availability: Research Only — preclinical stage for SNUC

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Enasidenib in IDH2-Mutated Malignant Sinonasal and Skull Base Tumors
biomarker_requirements: IDH2 mutation confirmed by molecular testing
clinical_trials.early_results: No results posted. Enasidenib 100 mg orally daily in 28-day cycles. Primary endpoint: PFS. Target enrollment: 25 patients. Initiated March 2024.

clinical_trials.title: Neoadjuvant and Adjuvant Pembrolizumab in Locally Advanced Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma
clinical_trials.early_results: Published NEJM 2025. Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab + cisplatin improved event-free survival vs standard of care in locally advanced HNSCC. May be extrapolated to SNUC.

clinical_trials.title: Pembrolizumab in Locally Advanced Sinonasal Carcinoma
clinical_trials.early_results: No results posted. Testing safety and activity of pembrolizumab + chemotherapy as neoadjuvant treatment for locally advanced sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma.

clinical_trials.title: Pembrolizumab + Nab-Paclitaxel + Cisplatin in Recurrent/Metastatic Sinonasal SCC
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 60%, DCR 100% in 20 patients with R/M SNSCC treated with pembrolizumab + nab-paclitaxel + cisplatin.

clinical_trials.title: Vorasidenib in IDH1/2-Mutant Low-Grade Glioma
clinical_trials.early_results: Median PFS 27.7 vs 11.1 months (HR 0.39, p<0.001). FDA approved for IDH-mutant grade 2 glioma. Provides rationale for off-label use in IDH2-mutant sinonasal tumors.

clinical_trials.title: Management of 80 Sinonasal Undifferentiated Carcinomas: Retrospective Multicentre Study (REFCOR)
sites: France (French Network of Rare Head and Neck Cancers)
clinical_trials.early_results: 80 SNUC patients treated across French REFCOR network. Provides contemporary treatment patterns and outcomes for European SNUC cohort.

combination_strategies.rationale: Response-adapted sequential approach: IC tests chemosensitivity; responders (67%) achieve 5-year OS 81% with CRT. Non-responders redirected to surgery. Ki-67 80% predicts chemosensitivity to platinum doublet

IDH2 inhibition epigenetically activates cGAS-STING pathway, increasing STING expression and type I IFN production. Promotes CD8+ T cell infiltration via CXCL10/CCL5, converting cold tumors to hot. Anti-PD-1 then unleashes infiltrating T cells. Mechanistically synergistic for IDH2-mutant tumors

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Phase 2 in AML (Dinardo 2021), rationale extrapolated to SNUC
combination_strategies.rationale: IDH2 mutation drives 2-HG accumulation causing DNA hypermethylation. Enasidenib blocks 2-HG production while azacitidine directly reverses existing hypermethylation. Dual epigenetic attack may restore differentiation in undifferentiated carcinoma. ORR 74% in IDH2-mutant AML

Case report of durable response in metastatic SNUC: after pembrolizumab monotherapy failure, addition of ipilimumab + celecoxib produced dramatic response. COX-2 inhibition reduces immunosuppressive prostaglandin E2 in tumor microenvironment, potentially enhancing ICI efficacy

SNUC-specific preclinical data demonstrates enhanced NK-cell killing with combined anti-PD-L1 blockade and IL-15 superagonism. IL-15 activates and expands NK cells; PD-L1 blockade removes immune checkpoint suppression. Triple combination provides innate + adaptive immune activation

IDH2 mutation produces 2-HG that inhibits homologous recombination repair enzymes, creating BRCAness. PARP inhibitor exploits this HR deficiency. Concurrent cisplatin adds DNA crosslinks that require HR for repair — creating double synthetic lethality in IDH2-mutant tumors

supportive_care.approach: Early Nutritional Intervention and Dietitian Support
supportive_care.evidence: Systematic review and meta-analysis (Frontiers in Oncology 2024) showing early nutritional intervention improves outcomes in H&N cancer patients receiving chemoradiation
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced treatment interruptions, maintained weight, improved treatment tolerance, reduced mucositis severity. Malnutrition adversely affects survival, wound healing, and QoL. Enteral nutrition (PEG/NG) preferred when oral intake insufficient

supportive_care.approach: Oral Mucositis Prevention and Management Protocol
supportive_care.evidence: Over 90% of H&N cancer patients receiving cisplatin-based CRT experience severe mucositis. Sinonasal primaries associated with greater risk of moderate-severe mucositis. Evidence-based protocols include basic oral care, benzydamine, low-level laser therapy
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced pain, maintained oral intake, fewer treatment breaks, reduced opioid requirements. MASCC/ISOO guidelines recommend prophylactic approaches including cryotherapy, photobiomodulation, and growth factors

supportive_care.approach: Psychosocial Support and Quality of Life Management
supportive_care.evidence: Sinonasal cancer treatment causes significant facial disfigurement, visual changes, and functional deficits. Psychological distress documented in 30-50% of H&N cancer patients. Cognitive behavioral therapy and peer support groups show benefit
supportive_care.benefit: Improved treatment adherence, reduced depression/anxiety, better overall QoL scores. Particularly important for 45yo patient facing potential orbital exenteration and craniofacial surgery

supportive_care.approach: Exercise and Physical Rehabilitation During and After Treatment
supportive_care.evidence: Meta-analyses of exercise interventions in H&N cancer show improved fatigue, physical function, and QoL. ACSM guidelines recommend 150 min/week moderate aerobic activity during cancer treatment when feasible
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced cancer-related fatigue, maintained muscle mass during chemoradiation, improved cardiorespiratory fitness, potential survival benefit. Important for young (45yo) patient to maintain functional capacity through multimodal treatment

supportive_care.approach: Hearing Monitoring and Otoprotection During Cisplatin Therapy
supportive_care.evidence: 25% hearing impairment rate with cisplatin-based IC for SNUC (Amit 2019). Serial audiometric monitoring recommended. Sodium thiosulfate may provide otoprotection in select settings
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of ototoxicity allows dose modification or switch to carboplatin. Preserves hearing and communication ability. Especially relevant for working-age patient

supportive_care.approach: Ophthalmologic Monitoring for T4a Disease with Orbital Involvement
supportive_care.evidence: 49.6% of SNUC patients in international cohort had orbital involvement. T4a disease involves orbital periosteum. Regular ophthalmologic assessment during and after treatment critical for visual preservation
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of radiation-induced optic neuropathy, monitoring of orbital reconstruction, management of epiphora and visual changes. Guides decision on orbital preservation vs exenteration based on treatment response
